claims.text: The Moon really does smell like spent gunpowder, as Apollo 17 astronaut Jack Schmitt and others reported, despite lunar dust being chemically nothing like actual gunpowder.

evidence.quote:
When recalling the smell of the moon Apollo
17 Jack Schmidt said everyone's impression of
the smell was that of spent gunpowder.

And this is strange because the makeup
of lunar dust is completely different than
gunpowder Moon Regolith is made up of silicon
dioxide glass created by billions of years
of media impacts.

claims.rationale: Apollo astronauts including Schmitt repeatedly described the smell as 'spent gunpowder' once moondust contacted the cabin air. This is well documented in NASA debriefs and oral histories. The chemical-mismatch observation is also accurate — lunar regolith contains no nitrate compounds.

claims.text: The leading explanations for the moon's gunpowder smell are either a desert-rain ('petrichor') effect — moondust molecules releasing odorants on contact with humid cabin air — or solar-wind ions trapped in the dust outgassing when warmed.

evidence.quote:
The molecules trapped in the dry moon dust
may have been activated by contact with the
moist air in the lunar module just like
Patrick wore in the desert rain effect.

Another theory is solar winds hot Solar Winds
of hydrogen helium and other ions Buffett
the moon's surface and may become trapped in the
dust when the ions are dislodged such as
by an astronaut's footsteps then come in contact
with the warm air inside the lunar
module they evaporate.

claims.rationale: Both hypotheses are real candidates discussed by NASA scientists for the moondust scent. Neither is fully proven; the smell is partly explained by reactive surfaces freshly exposed to the LM atmosphere, but the precise mechanism remains an open question.

claims.text: Astronauts returning from spacewalks consistently report that 'space itself' smells like burnt metal or burnt meat, possibly from oxidation of metal molecules deposited on suits when they re-encounter humidified cabin air.

evidence.quote:
Astronauts returning from spacewalks have reported smelling
burnt metal or burnt meat and
no one really knows for sure what this is
but it could be molecules of metal leeching off
onto spacesuits then oxidizing when it comes
into contact with actual humidifier air.

claims.rationale: ISS astronauts have repeatedly described the post-EVA scent as seared steak / hot metal / welding fumes. The oxidation/dying-stars theories are real candidate explanations. The astronaut testimony itself is well attested.

claims.text: Venus's atmosphere — dense carbon dioxide laced with sulfuric-acid clouds — would smell like burnt matches and rotten eggs.

evidence.quote:
Venus has a thick
atmosphere mostly carbon dioxide.
There's also a heavy clouds of sulfuric acid.
That combination makes Venus smell like
a burnt match and rotten eggs.

claims.rationale: Venus's atmosphere is ~96.5% CO2 with H2SO4 cloud decks; the chemistry maps reasonably to a sulfur-dioxide / sulfuric-acid scent profile (burnt matches, rotten eggs). Direct nose-experience is impossible but the chemical inference is sound.

claims.text: Mars's surface and thin atmosphere together would smell like rusty metal mixed with the exhaust of a poorly tuned car — iron-oxide dust combined with methane, formaldehyde, carbon monoxide, and sulfur dioxide in the air.

evidence.quote:
Mars is often referred to as the red planet
because of the iron oxide on the surface.

But what atmosphere there is contains
methane formaldehyde carbon monoxide and sulfur
dioxide. The Martian sand smells like rusty
metal because that's when iron oxide is
combining the owners of the surface with the orders
of the air and Mars is probably going
to smell like the exhaust of an
old car with a bad catalytic converter.

claims.rationale: Mars dust is rich in iron oxides; the atmosphere's trace constituents include methane (debated source) and sulfur and CO compounds. The 'old-car exhaust' framing is a casual but reasonable smell extrapolation. Some specific compound ratios (formaldehyde) are less established than the host implies.

claims.text: Jupiter's atmosphere is layered, with each depth producing a different stench — ammonia (horse-urine) at the outer layer, sulfur compounds (skunk) in the middle, and hydrogen cyanide (toasted-almonds) deeper in.

evidence.quote:
Jupiter's atmosphere is five thousand kilometres
thick and actually has multiple layers
each with slightly different smells.
The outer layers have a lot of
ammonia which smells like horse urine.
As you move closer to the surface
the atmosphere becomes more sulfuric smelling like
skunk And horse urine.
The inner layers have a high concentration
of hydrogen cyanide which smells like toasted
almonds and skunk and horse
urine and rotten eggs.

claims.rationale: Jupiter's atmospheric layering with ammonia ice crystals at the top and progressively heavier hydrocarbons / sulfur / HCN deeper down is well established. The colorful odor mappings are accurate olfactory descriptors of those compounds.

claims.text: Uranus is plausibly the smelliest planet in the solar system because its cloud tops are made of hydrogen sulfide, the same gas responsible for the smell of rotten eggs and human flatulence.

evidence.quote:
Well the cloud tops of Uranus are made
of hydrogen sulfide that gas responsible for the
foul smell of rotten eggs and.
Human flatulence e

claims.rationale: A 2018 Nature Astronomy study (Irwin et al.) confirmed H2S as a major component of Uranus's upper cloud deck — this finding made headlines specifically for the 'Uranus smells like rotten eggs' angle. The host's claim is correct.

claims.text: Sagittarius B2, the dust cloud at the center of our galaxy, contains ethyl formate — the chemical responsible for the flavor of raspberries and the smell of rum — meaning the galactic core would smell pleasant.

evidence.quote:
the center of our galaxy is the
dust cloud Sagittarius B2 and in that cloud
scientists have discovered a substance called ethyl
for me which is the chemical
responsible for the flavor of raspberries
and the smell of rum.

claims.rationale: Ethyl formate was indeed detected in the Sagittarius B2 molecular cloud by Belloche et al. (2009); it is associated with raspberry flavor and rum aroma. Whether the cloud as a whole would 'smell pleasant' is more whimsical extrapolation, but the underlying detection and aroma mapping are real.
